topics.title: Stack & Queue
topics.story: A stack is like a stack of plates at a buffet — you always take from the top and put on the top (LIFO). A queue is like people waiting in line at a coffee shop — first in, first out (FIFO). These two structures are the backbone of function calls, browser history, BFS, and undo operations.
topics.analogy: Stack = undo history in VS Code (Ctrl+Z removes last action). Queue = print spooler (documents print in the order they were submitted).
topics.memoryAnchor: STACK: Last In, First Out — like a gun magazine, last bullet in shoots first. QUEUE: First In, First Out — like a fair queue.
topics.firstPrinciples: Stack: push/pop/peek are all O(1). Used when you need LIFO ordering or to 'remember where you came from' (recursion, DFS, expression parsing). Queue: enqueue/dequeue are O(1). Used when FIFO ordering matters (BFS, task processing). Deque supports both.
topics.bruteForce: For balanced parentheses: count opens and closes — fails for mismatched types. For next greater element: nested loops O(n²). For BFS: recursion (wrong — creates DFS order). Always think whether LIFO or FIFO is the right ordering for your problem.
topics.optimizedApproach: Monotonic stack for next greater/smaller element in O(n). Deque for sliding window maximum in O(n). Two-stack trick for queue implementation. Stack to convert recursive DFS to iterative.
topics.whenToUse: Stack: DFS, expression evaluation, balanced parentheses, undo, call stack simulation. Queue: BFS, producer-consumer, rate limiting, task scheduling. Deque: sliding window max/min, palindrome check.

examples.title: Balanced Parentheses — Stack LIFO Match
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"for (char c : s.toCharArray())","variables":{"s":"({[]})","stack":"[]"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Process '({[]})' character by character."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"stack.push(c);","variables":{"c":"(","stack":"['(']"},"phase":"PUSH","annotation":"c='(': open bracket → push. Stack: ['(']."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"stack.push(c);","variables":{"c":"{","stack":"['(','{']" },"phase":"PUSH","annotation":"c='{': push. Stack: ['(', '{']."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"stack.push(c);","variables":{"c":"[","stack":"['(','{','[']" },"phase":"PUSH","annotation":"c='[': push. Stack: ['(', '{', '[']."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"char top = stack.pop();","variables":{"c":"]","top":"[","match":true},"phase":"POP","annotation":"c=']': pop top='['. ']' matches '[' ✓. Stack: ['(', '{']."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"char top = stack.pop();","variables":{"c":"}","top":"{","match":true},"phase":"POP","annotation":"c='}': pop top='{'. Match ✓. Stack: ['(']."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"char top = stack.pop();","variables":{"c":")","top":"(","match":true},"phase":"POP","annotation":"c=')': pop top='('. Match ✓. Stack: []."},{"line":14,"lineCode":"return stack.isEmpty();","variables":{"stack":"[]","result":true},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Stack empty → all brackets paired and nested correctly. Return true."}]
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION isValid(s):
  stack = empty stack
  FOR each char c in s:
    IF c is open bracket ('(', '{', '['):
      stack.push(c)
    ELSE:  // close bracket
      IF stack is empty: RETURN false
      top = stack.pop()
      IF c and top don't match: RETURN false
  RETURN stack.isEmpty()  // no unpaired opens
examples.explanation: Stack is perfect here because the most recently opened bracket must be the first to close (LIFO = Last In First Out = exactly how nesting works). Open brackets push. Close brackets pop and check for a match. Two failure modes: (1) close bracket with empty stack (no matching open), (2) mismatch at pop. Stack not empty at the end = unpaired open brackets. O(n) time, O(n) space.
examples.realWorldUse: HTML/XML tag validation. Compiler syntax checking (every { } in Java/C/Go). Expression evaluation (parentheses in math). IDE bracket matching highlighting. JSON/YAML linting. The stack pattern generalizes to: evaluate expressions (Reverse Polish Notation), implement undo/redo, DFS tree traversal without recursion.

examples.title: Next Greater Element — Monotonic Stack
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":5,"lineCode":"Deque<Integer> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();","variables":{"nums":"[4,1,2,3]","result":"[-1,-1,-1,-1]","stack":"[]"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Monotonic decreasing stack stores indices. We want: for each element, the first element to its RIGHT that is GREATER."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"while (!stack.isEmpty() && nums[stack.peek()] < nums[i])","variables":{"i":0,"nums[0]":4,"stack":"[]"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"i=0: stack empty. Push index 0. Stack: [0] (value 4)."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"while (!stack.isEmpty() && nums[stack.peek()] < nums[i])","variables":{"i":1,"nums[1]":1,"stack":"[0]"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"i=1: nums[0]=4 >= nums[1]=1, don't pop. Push 1. Stack: [0,1] (values 4,1)."},{"line":10,"lineCode":"result[idx] = nums[i];","variables":{"i":2,"nums[2]":2,"popped":1,"result[1]":2},"phase":"POP","annotation":"i=2: nums[1]=1 < nums[2]=2 → pop idx 1. result[1]=2. nums[0]=4 ≥ 2 → stop. Push 2."},{"line":10,"lineCode":"result[idx] = nums[i];","variables":{"i":3,"nums[3]":3,"popped":"2,then","result":"[-1,2,3,-1]"},"phase":"POP","annotation":"i=3: nums[2]=2 < 3 → pop idx 2, result[2]=3. nums[0]=4 ≥ 3 → stop. Push 3."},{"line":15,"lineCode":"return result;","variables":{"result":"[-1,2,3,-1]","remaining_stack":"[0,3]"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Stack has indices [0,3] (values 4,3): no greater element to right → they keep -1. O(n) total — each index pushed and popped at most once."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["nums=[4,1,2,3]"] --> B["i=0: push 0. Stack:[0|val4]"]
    B --> C["i=1: 1<4, push 1. Stack:[0,1|4,1]"]
    C --> D["i=2: 2>1, pop 1→result[1]=2. Stack:[0|4]"]
    D --> E["push 2. Stack:[0,2|4,2]"]
    E --> F["i=3: 3>2, pop 2→result[2]=3. Stack:[0|4]"]
    F --> G["push 3. result=[-1,2,3,-1] ✓"]
    style G fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION nextGreater(nums):
  result = array of -1s
  stack = []  // monotonic decreasing (stores indices)

  FOR i = 0 to n-1:
    // nums[i] is the 'next greater' for all smaller elements
    WHILE stack not empty AND nums[stack.top] < nums[i]:
      idx = stack.pop()
      result[idx] = nums[i]
    stack.push(i)

  // Elements left in stack have no next greater → -1
  RETURN result
examples.explanation: Monotonic stack maintains a decreasing order of values. When nums[i] breaks that order (is larger than top), the top element has found its 'next greater' element. Pop and record. The insight: we defer recording the answer until we see something larger — we don't know the next greater for nums[j] until we encounter it. O(n) — each element is pushed and popped at most once.
examples.realWorldUse: Stock span problem (days until stock surpasses today's price). Building temperatures (LeetCode 739). Largest rectangle in histogram. Rain water trapping. Daily temperatures. Any problem asking 'first larger/smaller element to the left/right'. Monotonic stack is the go-to O(n) pattern for these.

examples.title: Min Stack — O(1) getMin with Auxiliary Stack
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":5,"lineCode":"stack.push(val);","variables":{"val":5,"stack":"[5]","minStack":"[5]"},"phase":"PUSH","annotation":"Push 5. minStack empty → push 5 to minStack. Current min=5."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"stack.push(val);","variables":{"val":3,"stack":"[3,5]","minStack":"[3,5]"},"phase":"PUSH","annotation":"Push 3. 3 ≤ minStack.peek(5) → push 3. Min=3."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"stack.push(val);","variables":{"val":7,"stack":"[7,3,5]","minStack":"[3,5]"},"phase":"PUSH","annotation":"Push 7. 7 > 3 → don't push to minStack. Min still 3."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"int removed = stack.pop();","variables":{"removed":7,"stack":"[3,5]","minStack":"[3,5]"},"phase":"POP","annotation":"Pop 7. 7 ≠ minStack.peek(3) → don't touch minStack. Min still 3."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"int removed = stack.pop();","variables":{"removed":3,"stack":"[5]","minStack":"[5]"},"phase":"POP","annotation":"Pop 3. 3 == minStack.peek(3) → pop minStack. Min reverts to 5."},{"line":19,"lineCode":"getMin()","variables":{"minStack.peek":5},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"getMin()=5. O(1) because minStack.peek() always holds current minimum."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["push(5)"] --> B["stack:[5] minStack:[5]"]
    B --> C["push(3)"] --> D["stack:[3,5] minStack:[3,5]"]
    D --> E["push(7)"] --> F["stack:[7,3,5] minStack:[3,5]\n7>3 so don't update minStack"]
    F --> G["pop()=7"] --> H["stack:[3,5] minStack:[3,5]\n7≠3 so minStack unchanged"]
    H --> I["pop()=3"] --> J["stack:[5] minStack:[5]\n3==3 so pop minStack too"]
    J --> K["getMin()=5 O(1) ✓"]
    style K fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
CLASS MinStack:
  stack = []       // main stack
  minStack = []    // tracks all historical minimums

  push(val):
    stack.push(val)
    IF minStack empty OR val <= minStack.top:
      minStack.push(val)

  pop():
    removed = stack.pop()
    IF removed == minStack.top:
      minStack.pop()

  getMin(): RETURN minStack.top  // O(1)
examples.explanation: The challenge: stack is LIFO so we can't just store one global minimum (it changes as elements are popped). Auxiliary minStack: it maintains a decreasing sequence of minimums. Every time we push a new minimum (≤ current), we record it. When we pop an element that was the minimum, the previous minimum is now on top of minStack — instant O(1) retrieval. All operations O(1) time, O(n) space.
examples.realWorldUse: Text editor maintaining 'minimum undo depth' for redo optimization. Stock market: tracking minimum price during a price sequence. Database query optimizer: tracking minimum cost for a plan sequence. Any stateful system where you need O(1) access to the minimum/maximum of current state. Extends to max stack, median stack (two heaps).

examples.title: BFS Template — Shortest Path in Unweighted Grid
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":7,"lineCode":"queue.offer(start); visited[start[0]][start[1]] = true;","variables":{"start":"[0,0]","end":"[2,2]","queue":"[[0,0]]","steps":0},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"BFS start: enqueue [0,0], mark visited. Grid: 3x3 with no walls."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"int size = queue.size();","variables":{"size":1,"steps":0},"phase":"LEVEL_0","annotation":"Level 0 (steps=0): 1 node to process. Check if [0,0]==end? No. Enqueue neighbors [0,1] and [1,0]."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"int size = queue.size();","variables":{"size":2,"steps":1,"queue":"[[0,1],[1,0]]"},"phase":"LEVEL_1","annotation":"Level 1 (steps=1): 2 nodes. Process [0,1] → enqueue [0,2],[1,1]. Process [1,0] → enqueue [1,1](visited!),[2,0]."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"int size = queue.size();","variables":{"size":3,"steps":2,"queue":"[[0,2],[1,1],[2,0]]"},"phase":"LEVEL_2","annotation":"Level 2 (steps=2): 3 nodes. Process [0,2] → enqueue [1,2]. Process [1,1] → enqueue [2,1]. Process [2,0] → enqueue [2,1](dup!)."},{"line":15,"lineCode":"if (curr[0]==end[0] && curr[1]==end[1]) return steps;","variables":{"steps":4,"curr":"[2,2]"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Step 4: reach [2,2]==end. Return 4. BFS guarantees this is the MINIMUM number of steps."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["Start [0,0] steps=0"] --> B["BFS Level 1: [0,1],[1,0] steps=1"]
    B --> C["BFS Level 2: [0,2],[1,1],[2,0] steps=2"]
    C --> D["BFS Level 3: [1,2],[2,1] steps=3"]
    D --> E["BFS Level 4: [2,2]=END steps=4"]
    E --> F["Return 4 ✓"]
    style F fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION shortestPath(grid, start, end):
  queue = Queue()
  visited = 2D boolean array
  queue.add(start); visited[start] = true
  steps = 0
  dirs = [up, down, left, right]

  WHILE queue not empty:
    size = queue.size()    // current level count
    FOR i = 0 to size-1:
      curr = queue.poll()
      IF curr == end: RETURN steps
      FOR each direction d:
        next = curr + d
        IF valid AND not visited AND not wall:
          visited[next] = true
          queue.add(next)
    steps++
  RETURN -1  // unreachable
examples.explanation: BFS explores nodes level by level — all nodes at distance d before any at distance d+1. This guarantees the FIRST time we reach the destination, steps is the minimum. The `visited` array prevents cycles and redundant processing. Marking visited at enqueue time (not poll time) is critical — prevents adding the same cell multiple times. O(m×n) time and space.
examples.realWorldUse: Google Maps shortest route (unweighted). Word ladder (BFS on word graph). Minimum knight moves on chessboard. Shortest path in maze (0-1 BFS or regular BFS). Infection spread modeling. Social network: degrees of separation. Any 'minimum steps/hops/operations' problem where all edges have equal cost.

examples.title: Sliding Window Maximum — Monotonic Deque
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":5,"lineCode":"Deque<Integer> deque = new ArrayDeque<>();","variables":{"nums":"[1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7]","k":3},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Monotonic deque: front=max, back=smallest. Stores indices."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"while nums[deque.peekLast()] < nums[i]","variables":{"i":1,"nums[1]":3,"deque":"[0]","nums[0]":1},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"i=1: nums[0]=1 < nums[1]=3 → remove index 0 (1 can never be max in window containing 3). Add 1. Deque:[1]."},{"line":15,"lineCode":"deque.offerLast(i);","variables":{"i":2,"nums[2]":-1,"deque":"[1,2]"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"i=2: -1 < 3 so don't remove. Add 2. Deque:[1,2]. Window [1,3,-1] full. max=nums[1]=3."},{"line":8,"lineCode":"deque.peekFirst() < i - k + 1","variables":{"i":4,"k":3,"deque":"[4]","nums[4]":5},"phase":"EVICT","annotation":"i=4: deque had [2,3]. 5>-3 and 5>-1 → remove both. Add 4. Check front: 2 < 4-3+1=2? No. Window [−1,−3,5] max=5."},{"line":18,"lineCode":"result[i-k+1] = nums[deque.peekFirst()];","variables":{"result":"[3,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7]"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Final result: [3,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7]. Wait, result=[3,3,-1,-3,5,3,6] is wrong — correct: [3,3,5,5,6,7]. Deque front always holds current window max in O(1)."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["nums=[1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7] k=3"] --> B["i=0: deque=[0]"]
    B --> C["i=1: 3>1, remove 0. deque=[1]"]
    C --> D["i=2: window full. max=nums[1]=3"]
    D --> E["i=3: -3<3, add. deque=[1,2,3]. max=3"]
    E --> F["i=4: 5>all, clear deque. deque=[4]. max=5"]
    F --> G["i=6: 6>all, clear. max=6"]
    G --> H["result=[3,3,5,5,6,7] ✓"]
    style H fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION maxSlidingWindow(nums, k):
  deque = ArrayDeque()   // monotonic: front=max
  result = []

  FOR i = 0 to n-1:
    // 1. Evict out-of-window elements from front
    WHILE deque.front < i-k+1: deque.pollFront()

    // 2. Maintain decreasing order: remove smaller from back
    WHILE deque not empty AND nums[deque.back] < nums[i]:
      deque.pollBack()

    deque.addBack(i)

    // 3. Window full: max is front
    IF i >= k-1: result.add(nums[deque.front])

  RETURN result
examples.explanation: Monotonic deque maintains decreasing order of values (front = max, back = smallest). Key insight: if nums[j] < nums[i] and j < i, nums[j] will never be the window maximum again (nums[i] is both larger and will outlast it in the window). So we can remove nums[j]. Deque stores indices to handle eviction of out-of-window elements. Each element is added and removed at most once → O(n) total.
examples.realWorldUse: Real-time stream max with bounded memory. Moving average with maximum (financial signals). Sliding window maximum in audio/video processing. Task queue with time-bounded priority. The pattern of 'maintain monotonic order + evict old elements' appears in: Jump Game VI (DP + deque), constrained subsequence sum, maximum of minimum for every window size.

problems.title: Valid Parentheses
problems.description:
Given a string containing only '(', ')', '{', '}', '[', ']', determine if the input string is valid.

Valid means: open brackets are closed by the same type in the correct order.

**Example:**
Input: "()[]{}" → true
Input: "([)]" → false
Input: "{[]}" → true
problems.hint1: When you see an open bracket, what should you do? When you see a close bracket, what should you check?
problems.hint2: Push open brackets onto the stack. When you see a close bracket, pop and verify it matches the close bracket's corresponding open.
problems.hint3: Three checks: (1) stack empty when you see a close bracket → false, (2) top doesn't match → false, (3) stack not empty at end → false.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ s.length ≤ 10^4 | s consists of parentheses only
problems.editorial: Classic stack problem. O(n) time, O(n) space. The invariant: at every close bracket, the most recent unmatched open bracket must be the corresponding type. Stack naturally enforces this.

problems.title: Min Stack
problems.description:
Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum element in constant time.

- push(val): push element onto stack
- pop(): remove the top element
- top(): get the top element
- getMin(): retrieve the minimum element in the stack

All operations O(1).
problems.hint1: How can you track the minimum efficiently? Can you store extra information alongside each element?
problems.hint2: Use a second stack that tracks the minimum at each level. When you push val, push min(val, currentMin) onto the min stack.
problems.hint3: Both stacks stay in sync — push to both, pop from both. minStack.peek() is always the current minimum.
problems.testCases: [{"input":"push(-2), push(0), push(-3), getMin(), pop(), top(), getMin()","output":"-3, 0, -2"}]
problems.constraints: -2^31 ≤ val ≤ 2^31 - 1 | At most 3*10^4 calls | pop/top/getMin called only on non-empty stack
problems.editorial: Auxiliary min stack stores the running minimum at every level. Both stacks are always the same size. O(1) all operations, O(n) space. Alternative: store (val, minAtThisLevel) as pairs in one stack.

problems.title: Daily Temperatures
problems.description:
Given an array of integers temperatures, return an array answer where answer[i] is the number of days you have to wait after the ith day to get a warmer temperature. If there is no future day with a warmer temperature, answer[i] = 0.

**Example:**
Input: [73,74,75,71,69,72,76,73]
Output: [1,1,4,2,1,1,0,0]
problems.hint1: For each day, you need to find the nearest future day with a higher temperature. Brute force is O(n²). Can you do O(n)?
problems.hint2: Use a stack that stores indices of temperatures we haven't found a warmer day for yet. When does a waiting day get resolved?
problems.hint3: Maintain a monotonically decreasing stack of indices. When you see temperatures[i] > temperatures[stack.top()], the top index found its warmer day. answer[popped] = i - popped.
problems.editorial: Monotonic decreasing stack of indices. Each element is pushed once and popped once → O(n) amortized. When popped at index i, the wait = i - popped_index. Remaining stack elements have answer 0 (default array values).

problems.title: Implement Queue Using Two Stacks
problems.description:
Implement a FIFO queue using only two stacks. Supported operations: push(x), pop(), peek(), empty().

**Example:**
push(1), push(2), peek() → 1, pop() → 1, empty() → false
problems.hint1: Stack is LIFO, queue is FIFO. How can two stacks give you FIFO ordering?
problems.hint2: One stack for pushing (inbox), one for popping (outbox). When the outbox is empty and you need to dequeue, pour all inbox elements into outbox — this reverses the order to FIFO.
problems.hint3: Amortized O(1): each element is moved from inbox to outbox at most once. Only move when outbox is empty.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ x ≤ 9 | At most 100 calls to push, pop, peek, empty | Always valid (pop/peek on non-empty)
problems.editorial: Two-stack queue: inbox for writes, outbox for reads. Lazy transfer on read (only when outbox empty). Each element transferred at most once → amortized O(1) per operation. O(n) space.

problems.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
problems.description:
Given an array of integers heights representing the heights of bars in a histogram (width = 1 each), find the area of the largest rectangle in the histogram.

**Example:**
Input: [2,1,5,6,2,3]
Output: 10  (rectangle of height 5, width 2, between index 2–3)
problems.hint1: For each bar, the rectangle extends as far left and right as bars of equal or greater height. How do you find those boundaries efficiently?
problems.hint2: Use a monotonic increasing stack of indices. When you find a shorter bar, pop — the popped bar is the height, and the boundaries are determined by the current index and the new stack top.
problems.hint3: For each popped index h: height = heights[h], right_boundary = current_i, left_boundary = stack.isEmpty() ? 0 : stack.peek() + 1. Area = height * (right - left). Add sentinel 0 at end to flush all bars.
problems.editorial: Monotonic increasing stack. Key insight: for each bar as the height of a rectangle, extend as far as shorter bars allow. The stack gives us the left and right boundaries in O(1) per bar. O(n) time, O(n) space. Sentinel 0 forces the stack to flush at the end.

problems.title: Sliding Window Maximum
problems.description:
Given an integer array nums and a window size k, return the maximum of each window as it slides from left to right.

**Example:**
Input: nums = [1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7], k = 3
Output: [3,3,5,5,6,7]
problems.hint1: Brute force checks every window in O(k) → total O(nk). Can you maintain the maximum as you slide in O(1) per step?
problems.hint2: Use a deque (double-ended queue) to maintain a monotonically decreasing window of indices. The front is always the current maximum.
problems.hint3: For each new element i: (1) remove from back any indices with smaller values — they can never be the max while i is in the window. (2) Remove from front any indices outside the window (j <= i - k). (3) Front of deque = max of current window.
problems.editorial: Monotonic deque stores indices in decreasing order of values. Front = max of current window. Each element enqueued and dequeued at most once → O(n) total. Key: maintain window validity (remove front if out of range) AND maintain monotonicity (remove back if smaller than new element).

problems.title: Next Greater Element I
problems.description:
Given two arrays nums1 and nums2 (nums1 is a subset of nums2), find for each element in nums1 its next greater element in nums2. The next greater element of x is the first element to the right of x in nums2 that is greater than x. Return -1 if none.

**Example:**
nums1=[4,1,2], nums2=[1,3,4,2]
Output: [-1,3,-1] (4 has no greater, 1's next greater is 3, 2 has no greater)
problems.hint1: For each element in nums2, find its next greater element. Then answer queries from nums1 using a lookup.
problems.hint2: Process nums2 with a monotonic stack. When you find the next greater for element x, store it in a HashMap: x → nextGreater(x).
problems.hint3: For each element in nums1, look up the HashMap. If not present (means it's the maximum in its suffix), return -1.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ nums1.length ≤ nums2.length ≤ 1000 | 0 ≤ nums1[i], nums2[i] ≤ 10^4 | All elements unique
problems.editorial: Precompute NGE for all nums2 elements in O(n) using monotonic stack. Store in HashMap. Answer all nums1 queries in O(m) using lookup. Total O(m+n) vs O(m*n) brute force.

problems.title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
problems.description:
Evaluate the value of an arithmetic expression in Reverse Polish Notation (postfix). Valid operators: +, -, *, /. Integer division truncates toward zero.

**Example:**
Tokens: ['2','1','+','3','*'] → Output: 9 ((2+1)*3)
Tokens: ['4','13','5','/','+'] → Output: 6 (4+(13/5))
problems.hint1: In RPN, operators come after their operands. When you see an operator, apply it to the last two numbers seen. What data structure maintains the last seen numbers?
problems.hint2: Use a stack. For each token: if number → push. If operator → pop two, apply operator, push result.
problems.hint3: Pop order matters: first pop = right operand, second pop = left operand. So: b = pop(), a = pop(), push(a op b).
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ tokens.length ≤ 10^4 | Each token is an integer or one of +, -, *, / | Input is always a valid RPN expression | Result fits in 32-bit int
problems.editorial: Classic stack application. RPN was invented specifically so expressions can be evaluated with a single stack pass in O(n). Pop order: second pop is left operand, first pop is right operand (b = pop, a = pop, push a op b).

problems.title: Design Circular Queue
problems.description:
Implement a circular queue (ring buffer) with fixed capacity k.
- enQueue(val): inserts an element, returns false if full
- deQueue(): deletes the front element, returns false if empty
- Front(): gets the front item, -1 if empty
- Rear(): gets the last item, -1 if empty
- isEmpty(), isFull()

All operations O(1).
problems.hint1: Use an array of size k with two pointers: head (front) and tail (insertion point). How do you wrap around when you reach the end of the array?
problems.hint2: Use modulo arithmetic: tail = (tail + 1) % k when enqueuing. head = (head + 1) % k when dequeuing. Track size to distinguish full from empty.
problems.hint3: Size variable avoids the ambiguity of head==tail meaning full OR empty. Alternatively, use (tail+1)%k == head to detect full.
problems.testCases: [{"input":"MyCircularQueue(3): enQueue(1), enQueue(2), enQueue(3), enQueue(4), Rear(), isFull(), deQueue(), enQueue(4), Rear()","output":"true, true, true, false, 3, true, true, true, 4"}]
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ k ≤ 1000 | 0 ≤ value ≤ 1000 | At most 3000 calls to each operation
problems.editorial: Ring buffer with modulo arithmetic. size variable cleanly tracks count. Rear = (tail-1+k)%k to get last inserted element. Used in: OS I/O buffers, producer-consumer queues, audio streaming ring buffers.

problems.title: Implement Stack Using Queues
problems.description:
Implement a LIFO stack using only queue operations. Supported: push(x), pop(), top(), empty().

**Example:**
push(1), push(2), top()→2, pop()→2, empty()→false
problems.hint1: A queue is FIFO. How can you make the newest element accessible from the front?
problems.hint2: After pushing element x: rotate the queue so x moves to the front. Enqueue x, then dequeue and re-enqueue all previous elements (size-1 times).
problems.hint3: After rotation, the newly pushed element is at the front — pop() and top() work naturally from the front.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ x ≤ 9 | At most 100 calls | Always valid (pop/top on non-empty)
problems.editorial: After each push, rotate queue so new element is at front. O(n) push, O(1) pop/top. Symmetric to Queue-with-2-Stacks. Rotation trick: enqueue x, then dequeue-and-reenqueue all n-1 previous elements.
